news_communities.title: ‘Horrific tragedy': New details in case of high school student charged with murdering Danvers woman
news_communities.author: Kaitlin McKinley Becker, Munashe Kwangwari and Marc Fortier
news_communities.summary:
An 18-year-old from Lynn, Massachusetts, was ordered held without bail on Friday after he allegedly murdered a woman insider her Danvers home in what authorities believe was a random attack.

The Essex County District Attorney’s Office announced Thursday night that Anthony DeMayo, a senior at Bishop Fenwick High School in Peabody, was being charged with murder in connection to the death of 68-year-old Janet Swallow.

DeMayo was arraigned Friday afternoon in Salem District Court. He pleaded not guilty and was ordered held without bail. He’ll be sent to Bridgewater State Hospital for a mental evaluation and is scheduled to return to court next month.

The case was called briefly just before 10:30 a.m., but the judge ordered DeMayo to be evaluated first to determine his competency.

When DeMayo returned to court for his arraignment later in the day, court psychologist Joyce Perotta told the judge she had interviewed DeMayo for about an hour with his lawyer present. She said he presented with depressive symptoms, including restricted affect, anhedonia and suicidal ideation, and it appears those symptoms have existed for some time.

Perotta said DeMayo demonstrated “adequate understanding” of the court proceedings but she had concerns about his rational understanding and the potential impact of his current mental state on his legal decision making.

She recommended sending him to Bridgewater State Hospital under tight security for further assessment.

The judge agreed with Perotta’s assessment and ordered DeMayo to be held without bail and sent to Bridgewater State pending a pretrial hearing on April 1. His attorney did not object to the order.

The investigation that led to DeMayo’s arrest began after a 911 caller told police that a man was acting erratically, walking around on Standish Road in Lynn with what looked like a blood-stained knife. Officers responded and found DeMayo, taking him to Salem Hospital.

DeMayor reportedly told Lynn police that he had killed a woman the night before in Danvers.

A search of DeMayo’s Lynn home revealed blood-stained clothing, and along with data from his cell phone, investigators contacted the Danvers Police Department, who conducted a well-being check and found Swallow dead inside her home at 17 Amherst St.

Prosecutors said Swallow was found dead, alone, “suffering wounds consistent with a homicide.” They said DeMayo apparently entered the home by climbing through a window and stabbed Swallow while she was in bed. They said no items were found to be missing from the home and they believe the attack was random.

The longtime Danvers resident was an ICU nurse at Lahey Hospital in Burlington and leaves behind two sons.

Scott James, Lahey’s chief nursing officer, released a statement Friday calling Swallow “a beloved and valued member of the Lahey Hospital & Medical Center community” who will be “greatly missed.”

“We share our deepest condolences with her family and loved ones and her colleagues in the Nursing Department and across our hospital. Our thoughts are with them during this very difficult time,” he added.

Officials said there is no known connection between DeMayo and Swallow. No other details about what happened have been shared by authorities.

Bishop Fenwick High School President Tom Nunan Jr. issued a statement Friday morning about the murder:

“We are aware of the investigation, and the information released regarding that investigation, by the Danvers Police Department and the Essex County District Attorney’s Office.

We know this matter involves serious charges against an 18-year-old student who is in the custody and care of the proper authorities.

The reports reveal the incident took place off campus, did not involve any other members of our school, did not seem to indicate any connection between the victim and the person being charged with the crime, and does not present any threat to the Bishop Fenwick community.

We are cooperating fully with local law enforcement in this investigation, which is ongoing. Because this is an active criminal investigation, we defer to the appropriate legal authorities to provide any additional information. We will not have further comment on the matter.

Our thoughts and prayers are with the victim, her family, and all who have been affected by this horrific tragedy.

As a community of faith and hope, our focus is on our faculty and staff, our families, and especially our students. We are here to help them, to provide for them the supports they need, and to be present for them in this terribly difficult moment.  To that end, additional counselors and other trained professionals will be on campus today and Monday.

Thank you for your understanding and your prayer.“

Danvers Police Chief Jamie Lovell also issued a statement expressing his department’s “deepest condolences” to the Swallow family, her friends and everyone who knew her.

“The loss is being felt across our town, and many are struggling to make sense of it,” he said.

https://www.facebook.com/DanversPolice/posts/pfbid0E6dhsnqsKL63cmUHNtSEehVzsbJjEQ91PotC53NSq7zvJnnRhWKdYBxS2r8HDAe2l

news_communities.title: Danvers murder suspect was spotted wandering around Lynn before his arrest
news_communities.author: Amelia Fabiano
news_communities.summary:
A young man raised alarm on Thursday as he wandered streets and backyards in Lynn, Massachusetts, carrying a knife. Neighbors who saw him were shocked to learn that he’s accused in a murder in Danvers.

Multiple people told NBC10 Boston that they saw 18-year-old Anthony DeMayo walking around at about noon Thursday with a large knife in his hand. Later, he would be identified as the suspect in the murder of Janet Swallow in Danvers.

Several of them called 911, which led police to arrest him in a backyard on Standish Road. The whole thing was caught on home surveillance video.

“They said they saw a crazy guy with a knife, and I’m like, ‘How did this happen,’ you know? It’s scary, you never think, where you live, it’s going to happen,” John Bossi said.

Security cameras captured the moments leading up to the arrest. It starts with a vehicle crashing into Nells Pond in Lynn around 11:40 a.m.

“It’s hard to even get there. I don’t know how he got through. There’s no easy way for a car to get there,” Bossi said.

About 13 minutes later, DeMayo is seen walking down Bow Ridge Road in a bright green shirt, swinging a knife around in his right hand.

Then, he goes to Nells Pond Road. Ashley O’Brien was outside with her ducks when she saw him walk by.

“I thought for sure that this guy was obviously mentally unwell. I didn’t want to make a scene and run away or anything like that, cause he made eye contact with me,” she said.

She immediately called 911, the first of several calls from the neighborhood.

“I just waited till he passed my house, went in, told my husband and called the cops,” O’Brien said. “When we came back out, he was just kind of stabbing all the bushes outside and running a knife across the houses and stuff like that — it was very bizarre.”

Rhonda Deparolesa was another on nearby Standish Road.

“I was sitting on my chair, in the recliner, and all of the sudden, I see somebody jump over my fence,” Deparolesa said. “He just laid flat on his back on the bulkhead and just wasn’t moving, so my husband was upstairs, and I said, ‘Something’s going on here.'”

Deparolesa said he tried to open her door at one point, but thankfully, it was locked.

“What if he did get in my house? My husband and I might not be here,” she said. “You know, you think you’re safe in your house. Sometimes you’re not.”

By around 12:30 p.m., police were after him. They found him in a backyard a few doors down from Deparolesa’s home. He was arrested and placed in a police cruiser.

“I’m just absolutely shocked that a young kid, 18 years old, had his whole life ahead of him, and had to go and do this,” Deparolesa said. “For what? Why?”

Everyone who spoke to NBC10 Boston was shocked to learn DeMayo is accused of murder.

“I just thought this guy needed some help and I just called. I did not think that he’d just murdered somebody,” O’Brien said.

Police confirmed DeMayo was a student at Bishop Fenwick High School in Peabody. They did not comment on the investigation, but said they are fully cooperating with local law enforcement.

Their thoughts and prayers are on the victim, her family, and everyone impacted by the horrific tragedy. They said their focus right now is on staff and students.

They had additional counselors on campus Friday and will on Monday, as well.

news_communities.title: Falling bricks from Chelsea triple decker left vacant after fire
news_communities.author: Matt Fortin
news_communities.summary:
Bricks fell onto the sidewalk off a vacant building in Chelsea, Massachusetts, and emergency crews have created a collapse zone in the area as a precaution.

The Chelsea Fire Department said that it responded to the 120 block of Washington Avenue on Monday night for a report of bricks falling from a building. It was a triple decker that had been vacant since a serious fire there in January.

First responders originally heard that a pedestrian may have been hit by a falling brick, but they didn’t find anyone on scene.

Crews said a section of exterior brick wall over the second floor window collapsed onto the sidewalk below.

The city’s inspectional services determined the building was unsafe and created a collapse zone in the area until the owner can fix up the building.

Fire officials said the sidewalk and a section of Washington Avenue is closed between Orange Street and Addison Street. Washington Ave. is open to vehicle traffic so the MBTA 111 bus isn’t impacted, officials added.

news_communities.title: Falling bricks from Chelsea triple decker left vacant after fire - NBC Boston
news_communities.summary: Falling bricks from Chelsea triple decker left vacant after fire  NBC Boston

news_communities.title: Man dead after fire in Salem, Mass.
news_communities.author: Marc Fortier, Thea DiGiammerino and Jericho Tran
news_communities.summary:
A man has died after a fire in Salem, Massachusetts, on Tuesday.

Salem Fire Chief Alan Dionne said they were called to 18 Roslyn St. around 1:13 a.m. Firefighters pulled one person from a third-floor unit. That 62-year-old man was taken to Mass General Brigham in Salem, where he died of his injuries.

No other injuries were reported.

Dionne said there were hoarding conditions in the home that made a rescue more challenging for firefighters.

“It was incredibly difficult to find the victim, there was so much stuff in the building,” Dionne said.

John Payne, a former deputy chief with the Salem Fire Department, suited up and ran straight into the smoke-filled home just six weeks into his retirement.

“When I got to the third floor is where I started feeling a little bit of heat coming in because the door was wide open,” Payne said. “I had an old jacket of mine, so I grabbed that and a flashlight, and I went back up with one of the detectives, and we had an extinguisher, and I said ‘You ready?'”

Joined by a police detective, he ran in and attempted to save the man trapped on the third floor.

“Shirt over his face, we took one step in, and I go, ‘Damn, we’re not getting in here,'” Payne said.

Firefighters soon responded and replaced the duo, but were unable to save the man.

Dionne described their actions as “incredibly dangerous but obviously heroic.”

Aerial images showed several fire vehicles outside of a multi-story building, with a ladder extended to the roof.

The cause of the fire remains under investigation.

news_communities.title: New details revealed in court about Malden teacher accused of raping high school student
news_communities.author: Kaitlin McKinley Becker
news_communities.summary:
A 54-year-old teacher accused of raping a high school student in Malden, Massachusetts, faced a judge Thursday, a day after his arrest.

Authorities allege Scott Marino gave alcohol to an 18-year-old who is known to him and then sexually assaulted her last Wednesday, Feb. 18, at his Stoneham home, where the victim was currently living. Marino was arrested a week after the alleged incident and has been charged with two counts of rape, one count of indecent assault and battery and one count of furnishing alcohol to a person under the age of 21, according to the Middlesex County District Attorney’s Office.

Prosecutors provided shocking new details Thursday in Woburn District Court at the arraignment for Marino, who teaches at Linden STEAM Academy in Malden and coaches the girls’ basketball team at Malden High School.

Resources for victims of sexual assault are available through the National Sexual Violence Resources Center and the National Sexual Assault Telephone Hotline at 800-656-4673, and Massachusetts provides this list of statewide and resources for sexual assault survivors.

Jacob McCrindle of the Middlesex County District Attorney’s Office said in court that the alleged victim in this case had been in the foster care of Marino and his wife at the time this occurred. The victim, who moved in with the Marinos at their Stoneham home in the fall, was a basketball player for Marino during the season, and the two were known to each other for many years.

According to prosecutors, Marino’s wife was scheduled to take a trip out of state last Wednesday. The victim was on school vacation, so she went with Marino and a grandfather who lives in a basement apartment at the home to an Ash Wednesday mass. They dropped the older man off at home, and then Marino and the victim went driving because she had a learner’s permit and was learning to drive on the highway. They made the decision to go into Boston to get a takeout seafood dinner, and on the way home, Marino had them stop at a liquor store where surveillance video shows he purchased a strawberry margarita mix, a large bottle of Patron tequila and some other agave liquor.

The two then went back to Marino’s Stoneham home and he allegedly gave her a large margarita. She drank some of it but didn’t like it, though Marino allegedly encouraged her to keep drinking. He later poured them shots of tequila, and they ate the seafood dinner they got. The victim later told police that she was feeling ill so she went to her bedroom where she vomited many times, eventually lying down in bed.

Marino then came in and sexually assaulted her two times, prosecutors said in court. The victim later retrieved her cell phone and began texting friends and ultimately an aunt, begging for help.

McCrindle said that the aunt called 911 as she was driving to the Marino’s home, and the victim was taken to Winchester Hospital for a full sexual assault examination.

“This is a relationship where the defendant was the victim’s foster parent, he took her into his home, that’s a position of trust, as well as coupled with the fact that the defendant is a teacher, or was a teacher at the Malden Public Schools and he was also her coach for many years,” McCrindle said in court, arguing for $100,000 cash bail. “The defendant used I would suggest that relationship, he took advantage of that relationship, there’s a significant age difference, the defendant is 54 years old, the victim is 18 years old. There’s also a significant size difference that’s described in the police report between the defendant and the alleged victim…. it’s also noteworthy that the defendant appears to have waited until his wife left the home so he was more isolated with the victim…clearly some planning went into this.”

Marino’s defense attorney argued before the judge that he’s a respected educator with deep community support who has “never been in court in his life.”

The judge ultimately ordered $75,000 cash bail based on the gravity of circumstances along with several conditions requested by the commonwealth, including that Marino stay away and have no contact from the victim and any witnesses, stay away from all Malden school property and events, be fitted with a GPS bracelet to enforce exclusive zones including the victim’s home address, her school, her workplace and a gym that she frequents, surrender his passport and not be allowed to leave the state.

The judge also added the condition that Marino be alcohol free and submit to SCRAM testing.

Resources for victims of sexual assault are available through the National Sexual Violence Resources Center and the National Sexual Assault Telephone Hotline at 800-656-4673, and Massachusetts provides this list of statewide and resources for sexual assault survivors.

Malden Superintendent Timothy Sippel previously said the district was informed Wednesday that Marino had been taken into custody in connection with “a serious incident alleged to have occurred outside of working hours and involving one of our high school students.”

Marino was immediately placed on leave, the superintendent added, noting that the district is cooperating fully with law enforcement agencies in their investigation.

“I understand how upsetting and distressing this is to our community given the serious nature of these allegations,” Sippel said in a statement. “The safety of our students and staff is our highest priority.”

A plea of not guilty was entered on Marino’s behalf. He is due back in court for a probable cause hearing on April 16.

news_communities.title: Malden teacher and coach charged with rape of teen
news_communities.author: Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
A teacher and coach in Malden, Massachusetts, has charged with the rape of an 18-year-old.

Malden Public Schools said Scott Marino had been arrested over a “serious incident” involving a high school student. The Middlesex County District Attorney’s Office and Stoneham Police Department later said he had been charged with two counts of rape, one count of indecent assault and battery and one count of furnishing alcohol to a person under 21.

Marino is accused of providing alcohol to the victim, who is known to him and sexually assaulting her on Feb. 18, prosecutors said.

Superintendent Timothy Sippel said Marino teaches at Linden STEAM Academy — which serves kindergarten through eighth grade — and is the head coach of the Malden High School girls’ basketball team.

Sippel said that Marino’s “arrest is in connection with a serious incident alleged to have occurred outside of working hours and involving one of our high school students.”

“I understand how upsetting and distressing this is to our community given the serious nature of these allegations,” the superintendent said.

Marino is due to be arraigned Thursday in Woburn District Court. It was not immediately clear if he had an attorney.

news_communities.title: Girl rescued after falling through the ice in Malden
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino and Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
Firefighters rescued a teen girl who had fallen through the ice into the frigid water of a pond in Malden, Massachusetts, on Wednesday afternoon.

It happened around 2 p.m. at Fellsmere Pond, fire officials said. Firefighters in water rescue suits went in to find the girl holding onto the edge of the ice, according to the Malden Fire Department. She was pulled from the water in under 30 seconds.

“I don’t even think the engine stopped,” Malden Fire Captain Roy Tribble said. “I jumped up, I sprinted down here, pretty much just jumped right into the water.”

The 14-year-old girl was conscious and alert when she was brought to a Boston hospital for treatment.

Tribble said the water was about 30 feet deep.

“A life was saved. It was a young girl who fell through the ice,” added Captain Bob Hegarty.

He warned everyone to stay off thin ice.

“A warning to everybody out there that ice, if it’s not thick enough, you’re going to fall through, and if you fall through, you’re not going to be able to get yourself out,” Hegarty said. “So stay off the ice until it’s very thick, which — who can tell when it’s very thick?”

news_communities.title: Malden woman accused of threatening federal agents making immigration arrest
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_communities.summary:
A Massachusetts woman is accused of threatening to kill federal agents who were conducting immigration enforcement in Malden.

Bethany Abigail Terrill, 37, was charged with threatening a United States official, the U.S. Attorney’s Office said Thursday.

Prosecutors say that Terrill approached the agents as they were making an arrest outside Malden District Court on Sept. 29, describing her as verbally abusive. She allegedly shouted things like “ICE is here, ICE is here,” “You guys are monsters, this is insane,” “Sir, what’s your name, what’s your name,” “I can try to help you,” and “I am an American civilian, I have a right to be here,” and called the agents Nazis.

She is further accused of trying to push past the agents, yelling “Charlie Kirk died, and we love it… We’re coming for you, gonna kill you.” 

According to the statement of probable cause, an agent heard the threat and proceeded to detain Terrill, who they said was “actively resistant and fought to escape.”

Officials say the agents were wearing body cameras, and Terrill was also filming at the time. In the statement of probable cause, investigators say the threat can be heard in the footage from Terrill’s phone.

The agents on scene were from multiple agencies, including Homeland Security Investigations (HSI), ICE and the FBI.

During her appearance in U.S. District Court in Boston on Thursday, a public defender said Terrill owns a salon and was struggling with legal issues in an unrelated case.

Judge Paul Levenson ordered Terrill be released with conditions, saying “federal agents have a right to not be subjected to threats.”

Terrill was ordered as part of her conditions of release to participate in a mental health program for treatment on a regular basis, to develop a safety plan for her own safety and others, avoid contact with potential witnesses, have no contact with any federal official, family or otherwise in any way or form, refrain from alcohol and the use of any illegal drugs, to avoid contact with law enforcement, and to refrain from making any further threats.

She didn’t speak to reporters outside of court.

The charge of threatening a U.S. official carries a sentence of up to 10 years in prison, three years of supervised release and a fine of $250,000.

news_communities.title: Cities bordering Everett unite in Revolution soccer stadium transparency push
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
Four cities that border Everett, Massachusetts, have banded together in a push for more involvement in the New England Revolution’s plans for a soccer-specific stadium in the city.

The mayors of Revere, Medford, Malden and Chelsea formed a coalition called City Mayors for Citizens that they said will seek transparency on plans for the stadium, including the impacts it will have on their communities.

The 25,000-seat stadium proposal has progressed in the state Legislature but still faces plenty of hurdles. The City Mayors for Citizens said they are worried about how the building on along the Mystic River might impact local infrastructure, public transit and quality of life.

“A project of this size and scale will have significant regional impacts,” said Revere Mayor Patrick Keefe Jr said in a statement. “We want to ensure that a stadium is contributing positively to the communities it is drawing resources from. Our residents should not bear the burdens of a private stadium that has strong potential to negatively affect their day-to-day lives.”

Asked for a response to the statement, a Revolution club spokesperson noted that it expects to work with neighboring communities after it reaches agreements with Everett and Boston, which would allow the property to have its port designation removed and the process of construction to continue.

The spokesperson also noted that the team thanked the mayors and promised to review the concerns raised in their letter, sent Monday.

news_communities.title: Video shows Salvadoran man being detained by ICE in Malden
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain, Marc Fortier and Mark Garfinkel
news_communities.summary:
The wife of a Salvadoran man says he was violently taken into custody by immigration officials on his way to work in Malden, Massachusetts, on Wednesday morning.

The incident occurred shortly before 6 a.m. in the area of 28 Kennedy Drive.

Leslie Perlera Gonzalez said she received a distressing call from her husband, Edgar Hernan Elias Escobar, shortly before 6 a.m. as he was heading to his construction job. She rushed to Kennedy Drive, where she said Elias Escobar was surrounded by immigration officers.

“I heard them smash his window, and he was saying, ‘Ow, you’re hurting me,'” Perlera Gonzalez said. “When I arrived on the scene, he had a Taser on his face.”

The 31-year-old had no criminal record and was in the process of adjusting his immigration status after the couple married last year, noted Perlera Gonzalez.

“He’s never come close to doing anything that would be considered a crime,” she said.

She alleged that agents used an unmarked car to ram her husband’s vehicle, forcing him off the road before detaining him.

Malden police said Elias Escobar sustained minor injuries. He was transported on a stretcher to a hospital in Melrose, where Perlera Gonzalez said she was not allowed to see him.

About an hour later, he was moved to a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement processing facility in Burlington.

A spokesperson from the Department of Homeland Security said in a statement that Elias Escobar was the target of the ICE operation and suffered “no injuries.”

“On September 17, 2025, ICE conducted an immigration enforcement operation in Malden, Massachusetts to arrest Edgar Hernan Elias-Escobar, an illegal alien from El Salvador. During the arrest, the illegal alien refused to comply with officer commands to rolldown his window. The officers took appropriate action and followed their training to use the minimum amount of force necessary to resolve the situation in a manner that ensured the success of the operation and prioritized the safety of the public and our officers,” the spokesperson said in a statement. “Out of an abundance of caution, ICE law enforcement escorted Elias-Escobar to the hospital for evaluations. Medical staff found he had no injuries and discharged him. He was then escorted to the Boston Filed office for processing and will be placed in removal proceedings.”

“I’ve been expecting this from somebody I know every day since Donald Trump was elected,” said Amiee Parco, an attorney with Greater Boston Legal Services and a colleague of Perlera Gonzalez. “I’ve been waiting for it to affect me personally.”

Friends and coworkers gathered outside the Burlington facility, joining a weekly protest to support Elias Escobar’s release and that of other detainees.

“He’s a really wonderful person,” said Alyssa Rao, a friend of the couple. “I think this is going to impact everyone.”

After Wednesday’s ordeal, Perlera Gonzalez fears what her husband is going through while detained.

“I’m terrified of what could be happening when no one is watching,” she said.

At last check, Escobar was still at the facility. It’s unclear how long he’ll be held — often, people stay several days, though the facility is meant to detain people for hours.

His lawyer is trying to prevent the government from sending him to a far-away detention center, and from deportation.

ICE has ramped up arrests across greater Boston in recent weeks under “Operation Patriot 2.0,” detaining immigrants in public spaces.

Gov. Maura Healey spoke out against the sweeps on Tuesday, saying federal immigration enforcement officers are “creating an incredible amount of fear and intimidation.”

“Some of it seems purposeful, to be honest. Why else are you parked outside a church? Why are you parked outside a community center?”

The governor said her office has received no information from ICE about who is being detained or why.

“This is not a furtherance of public safety,” she said. “In fact, it undermines public safety.”

Asked what she can do to protest White House policy, Healey said: “I join with other governors in urging the Trump administration, in the strongest possible terms, to change course.”

State House News Service contributed to this report.

news_boston.title: Second teen arrested in deadly Chelsea stabbing
news_boston.author: Mike Pescaro, Sebastian Agudelo Gomez and Marc Fortier
news_boston.summary:
Police in Chelsea, Massachusetts, have arrested a second teenager three months after a stabbing that left a man dead.

Eduardo Rózales López, 25, of Chelsea, was killed in the March 19 stabbing near the intersection of Chestnut Street and Everett Avenue.

Sergio Josué Castellanos, an 18-year-old who suffered minor injuries in the incident, was ordered held without bail a day later when he was arraigned on a murder charge.

Chelsea police said Thursday that an unnamed 17-year-old had been arrested on a murder warrant.

The teen will be arraigned Friday in Chelsea District Court.

“I am very happy with today’s arrest and although an arrest can never replace the loss of a loved one, I hope this can give the family of Mr. Lopez further closure,” Chelsea Police Chief Keith Houghton said in a statement.

Prosecutors with the Suffolk County District Attorney’s Office said López was treated at the scene for stab wounds to his neck and torso. He was transported to Massachusetts General Hospital, where he died from his injuries.

Reviewing video, police described seeing five people chasing after López, prosecutors said at Castellanos’ arraignment.

A man identified as Castellanos could be seen striking López in a stabbing motion while he’s on the ground, prosecutors said.

The video allegedly showed Castellanos and three other people fleeing. Police found him and three other males behind the fence of a home nearby on Chestnut Street.

Castellanos, who was tending to a stab wound to his leg, had changed his clothes from those seen on camera, which were found next to him, prosecutors said. He was transported to an area hospital for treatment.

Authorities said a black machete with stains on the blade was recovered.

Castellanos’ defense attorney, asking that his client be released on bail with GPS monitoring, raised questions about the identity of the attacker. He noted that in the video in which prosecutors described Castellanos making a stabbing motion, no knife was seen.

The judge ordered Castellanos held without bail.

Family members told Telemundo Nueva Inglaterra that López arrived from Guatemala seven years ago and left behind a 2-year-old child.

People in the area were shocked by the confrontation.

“It was kind of scary, I’m not going to lie. You still see the remnants of blood on the floor,” neighbor Shauniece White said Thursday. “It’s extremely concerning. As you see, it’s only been one nice day so far. If it’s already happening now, I can only imagine what the summer has in store for us. I’m hopeful that the police will come out and do due diligence to keep the community safe, but wishful thinking in a time like this.”

Massachusetts State Police are assisting Chelsea police with the investigation.

Chelsea police ask anyone with information to call 617-466-4800 or submit an anonymous tip by calling 617-466-4880.

news_boston.title: Turkey stuns USA 3-2 with final kick of World Cup Group D finale
news_boston.author: Sanjesh Singh
news_boston.summary:
That’s not quite the way the U.S. men’s national team wanted to end the World Cup group stage.

With the final action of the game, Turkish defender Kaan Ayhan stabbed home from close range in he 98th minute to take all three points in a 3-2 win in Los Angeles.

Ayhan came off the bench in the 88th minute. The result didn’t matter, but the U.S. was seeking to go 3-0-0 for the first time ever in the World Cup group stage. But the Crescent-Stars claimed a moment of their own.

Head coach Mauricio Pochettino went with almost an entirely rotated side, making nine changes from the 2-0 win vs. Australia. Weston McKennie captained the side and was the only regular starter in the lineup, with Ricardo Pepi staying in attack in place of Folarin Balogun.

Turkey, on the other hand, came out strong with Arda Guler and Kenan Yildiz headlining the young stars. Orkun Kokcu and Baris Yilmaz also received deserved starts with their respective qualities.

Just like the first two games, the U.S. opened the score early in the first half. It wasn’t an own goal this time, however.

In just the third minute, a Sebastian Berhalter corner kick from the left flank found Auston Trusty at the back post. The makeshift left-back controlled it well before drilling it home with his left foot.

But the lead didn’t last for long.

Seven minutes later, Turkey scored its first goal of the tournament when Arda Guler got in behind Mark McKenzie and Miles Robinson.

The Real Madrid star had no issue smashing it home with his left foot past backup goalie Matt Turner, who came in for Matt Freese.

The momentum went back to the U.S. from there, but it struggled to create consistent pressure against Turkey with Pepi up top and Tim Weah and Brendan Aaronson on the wings.

Eventually, the first wave of Turkish possession resulted in a goal. Kenan Yildiz showed off his potential by opening the action up on the left flank, then playing it to left-back Eren Elmali. Elmali then cut it back inside the box, where midfielder Orkun Kokcu tapped home in the 31st minute.

It came at an inopportune time for the U.S. as McKenzie had just put the ball in the net a few minutes earlier, though offsides ruled it out. Turkey then dominated possession until halftime, but didn’t score.

The USMNT’s lack of backup quality was evident at the interval, as only Trusty and some flashes of Berhalter stood out among players not named McKennie. Pepi didn’t get involved enough and the wing creation lacked due to the wrong profiles operating there.

But the Stars and Stripes got on the board next — and it was Berhalter.

In just the 49th minute, the U.S. had a long throw from the left flank. McKenzie put it in towards the goal, but it was cleared just outside the box. That’s where Berhalter came to meet it. And meet it he did, drilling it with technique beyond goalie Ugurcan Çakir.

Christian Pulisic then came off the bench for Weah on the left wing in the 58th minute, which uplifted the home crowd even more.

He immediately got involved near the box and even had some close efforts, one that grazed the post and another outside the box that just went wide.

It came down to the final minutes. There were seven added minutes of stoppage time, but the winning goal came beyond that.

The U.S. will now move onto the Round of 32, where it will meet Bosnia and Herzegovina next Wednesday at San Francisco Bay Area Stadium. Kickoff time is set for 5 p.m. PT/8 p.m. ET.

Turkey, meanwhile, will go home after losing its first two games to Australia and Paraguay.

How the U.S. responds to this partial setback, even if the result doesn’t matter, will be vital. Turkey didn’t attack much in the second half and relied on transition plays to build momentum. The U.S. backups were trying to earn more minutes down the line, but most failed to impress.

The main starters will be back for Bosnia, so the hope will be to stay as healthy as possible to truly embrace a deep run.

news_boston.title: Mass. launches interactive housing database
news_boston.author: Matt Prichard
news_boston.summary:
Advocates are trying to turn the tides on the housing crisis in Massachusetts.

“We need to have data-driven conversations,” said Jesse Kanson-Benanav of Abundant Housing Massachusetts.

“It’s about that we are showing what direction we are going in,” said Brandon Stanaway of Allston-Brighton Housing Action.

The Healey administration launched a new housing database on Thursday allowing Bay Staters to click on their city and see progress in that specific spot.

“We need to start with one unit, one building at a time, and this gives us an opportunity to see where that’s happening,” said Stanaway.

The website includes data from all cities and towns across the state and shows Boston with 5,983 new homes, Norwood with 133 and Gloucester with 57.

The dataset is also new, using U.S. census totals of new addresses and showing 34,500 new homes in 2025, which amounts to 15% of the goal of 222,000 homes by 2035.

“It will help policymakers, municipalities, advocates like my members, really understand the scope of where we are going,” said Kanson-Benanav.

But Republican candidates for governor share a different perspective, arguing the numbers are not what they seem.

“We’re producing less housing on a per-capita basis than any other state,” said Republican gubernatorial candidate Brian Shortsleeve.

“On permits, we’re in the bottom tier,” said fellow GOP candidate Mike Minogue. “We are not seeing developers come in and even want to build.”

Democratic Gov. Maura Healey countered that view, writing in a statement, “This new data shows that we are making serious progress … we know there is more work to do.”

Meanwhile, advocates are encouraged to see movement urging all involved to continue seeking ways to create more housing in the commonwealth.

“We need to be realistic, also, about that progress,” said Kanson-Benanav.

“Every unit is a step in the right direction,” said Stanaway.

The housing data website launched on Thursday and can be viewed on the state’s website. The state plans to publish updates twice a year.

news_boston.title: She brought her late dad's vintage camera to her wedding. The film developed with a surprise
news_boston.author: Rachel Paula Abrahamson | TODAY
news_boston.summary:
For years, Mia Chard imagined what her wedding day would feel like without her dad.

Her father, Doug Chard, who died in 2018 at age 72 after a long illness, had been one of her closest companions.

Mia describes her father as “tough and tender.” Friends often thought he looked intimidating, she says, but he was patient, caring and one of the gentlest people she knew.

“I considered him one of my best friends,” Chard, a 43-year-old social worker in Utah, tells TODAY.com.

Growing up, her mother, Susana, owned a wedding business. They spent countless hours arranging flowers, decorating venues and helping other couples celebrate milestones, even as Chard wondered whether she would ever have one of her own.

“I felt very insecure about my ability to find a partner or have relationships,” she says.

She had spent most of her life single, something she spoke about openly online. One of those videos caught the attention of Sam Perez, a 41-year-old film and video editor. He reached out. She responded.

Before long, they were building a future together.

As she and Perez planned their May 2 wedding, memories of her father seemed woven into nearly every decision.

“There was so much of him in everything we were doing,” she says.

One choice felt especially important.

Her mother hoped one of Chard’s brothers would walk her down the aisle. Her oldest brother was already officiating the ceremony. But Chard couldn’t bring herself to replace the role she had always imagined her father filling.

“If it’s not Dad, it’s no one,’” she remembered thinking. “I’ll do it myself.’”

See Chard’s TikTok here: A Wedding Memory: My Dad’s Spirit Captured on Film.

Before the ceremony, she made sure to bring something that belonged to him: a camera.

It was a vintage Rollei film camera her father had purchased in the 1970s. He carried it through different chapters of his life.

After his death, Chard began bringing the camera to take photos at birthdays, family gatherings and trips, as “a way to keep him on adventures with us,” she says. She continued using it regularly.

On her wedding day, she handed it to one of her brothers and asked him to photograph whatever caught his eye. Then she forgot about it.

Nearly a month later, the developed images arrived by email.

Chard opened the gallery expecting wedding photographs. Instead, the first image left her stunned. It was a photo of her father and mother together … from an occasion years ago.

“And I’m like, ‘What is this?’”

As she continued scrolling, she realized something unusual had happened. Images from her wedding day had been overlaid with photographs from a family gathering decades earlier. The older images appeared to date back to her brother’s graduation in 1999.

At first, she was disappointed. Then the more she studied the photographs, the harder they were to dismiss as simply damaged film. Family members appeared layered across wedding scenes. In one image, Chard noticed how the overlapping exposures framed her as she walked down the aisle.

“It felt meant to be,” she says. “It truly felt meant to be.”

For Chard, who admits she has often been skeptical of assigning meaning to unusual events, the experience felt different. She believes in something beyond this life, she says, but she has also lived with doubt. Unlike some family members who speak openly about sensing her father’s presence, Chard says she rarely experiences those moments herself.

“There have been instances where I feel like he connects with me through music,” she says. “And I feel like he connects with me through pictures in some ways.”

The mystery of the film only deepened that feeling. Chard remembers purchasing new film and believing that was what had been loaded into the camera.

Although she had been using the camera for years without incident, Chard says she believed she had loaded a new roll of film several months before the wedding. Instead, when the images were developed, she realized the roll already contained photographs taken decades earlier.

“I just can’t explain how in the world that roll of film was in my camera,” she says.

Right before Chard stepped down the aisle, she thought about her father.

“I wanted him with me,” she says.

Weeks later, when she opened the gallery and saw her parents staring back her from the first photograph, she felt she had her answer.

“And he was.”

In a TikTok video, Chard reflected on the discovery, writing that it felt as though “the one person I had wanted at my wedding was trying to tell me he had been there all along.”

She believes her father was “right beside ‘his girl’” as she walked down the aisle.

“He’s alive in my memories, he’s alive in my heart,” she wrote. “The man who showed me how to love is alive in every act of love that comes from me.”

She ended the post with a simple conclusion:

“Of course he made it to my wedding day. I want to believe I have the pictures to prove it.”

This story first appeared on TODAY.com. More from TODAY:

  • She Brought Her Late Dad’s Vintage Camera to Her Wedding. The Film Developed With a Surprise
  • After Hundreds of Women Wrote In, ‘Date My Dad’ Bachelor Went on 3 Dates: Watch What Happened!
  • Mariska Hargitay Says Broadway Debut Was ‘Cripplingly Terrifying.’ She Hung These 4 Pictures in Her Dressing Room for Inspiration

news_boston.title: Flight from Boston to France returns over smoke in the cockpit, Delta says
news_boston.author: Asher Klein
news_boston.summary:
A flight to France had to return to Boston Thursday evening because of smoke in the cockpit, its airline said.

The plane, Delta Flight 276 to Nice, landed safely after declaring an emergency as a precaution, according to Delta. The A330-300 had 250 passengers on board, with three pilots and 10 flight attendants.

Emergency vehicles could be seen around the plane at the gate at Logan International Airport.

Massport, which operates the airport, said its fire department inspected the plane and that it taxied to its gate on its own power.

The plane turned around soon after it took off, according to flight-tracking data.

Delta said it was working to get passengers back to their destinations, with apologies for the inconvenience.

news_boston.title: USMNT to face Bosnia and Herzegovina in World Cup Round of 32
news_boston.author: Sanjesh Singh
news_boston.summary:
The U.S. men’s national team now knows its opponent for the Round of 32.

Up next in the World Cup on home soil is Bosnia and Herzegovina, the third-place finishers in Group B.

It’s an opponent that was widely known for a while, but needed final confirmation steps from FIFA before becoming official. Japan and Sweden’s 1-1 draw Thursday sealed it.

The game is set for Wednesday, July 1 at San Francisco Bay Area Stadium. Kick-off time is slated for 8 p.m. ET/5 p.m. PT.

The U.S. won Group D without kicking a ball. After a 4-1 win vs. Paraguay and a 2-0 victory vs. Australia, the U.S. claimed first place after Turkey lost its two games leading up to the group finale. Turkey then beat the U.S. 3-2 with the last kick of the game.

MORE WORLD CUP COVERAGE

2026 World Cup Jun 24

What we know about the Round of 32 World Cup schedule and teams so far

2026 World Cup Jun 22

USMNT in the World Cup semifinals? Why a former player believes

World Cup May 27

Where do the USMNT's World Cup stars play professionally? Here are their clubs

Bosnia and Herzegovina, a European country bordered alongside Croatia, Serbia and Montenegro, opened the tournament with a 1-1 draw to Canada. The Dragons lost 4-1 to Switzerland in the second game, but rebounded with a 3-1 win vs. Qatar to solidify their spot in the Round of 32.

The Dragons, ranked No. 62 by FIFA, made their first World Cup appearance in 2014 but failed to advance past the group.

The U.S. men’s national team made the Round of 16 in Qatar but fell to the Netherlands. The Stars and Stripes’ best finish came in the first ever World Cup in 1930, where it came in third out of 13 teams.

Bosnia is led by veteran defender Sead Kolasinac and 40-year-old striker Edin Dzeko, while winger Kerim Alajbegovic is breaking out alongside Esmir Bajraktarević, who was born in Appleton, Wisconsin.

The U.S. should have enough quality in each phase to get through with a win in front of its home support, but underdogs have been shining throughout the tournament. Head coach Mauricio Pochettino will need to balance his team well.

news_boston.title: 2 customers hospitalized after SUV crashes into Newburyport restaurant
news_boston.summary:
An SUV crashed into a restaurant Thursday afternoon in Newburyport, Massachusetts, injuring three diners.

Police responded shortly before 6:30 p.m. to the Black Cow on Merrimac Street, where a BMW X3 had crashed through a wall of floor-to-ceiling windows near the entrance.

Two men who were inside the restaurant were taken by ambulance to Anna Jaques Hospital in Newburyport to be treated for injuries. A third customer was also hurt, but declined to be transported.

None of the victims’ injuries are believed to be life-threatening, police said.

The driver, a 67-year-old Groveland woman, was not injured in the crash and is cooperating with police.

Alcohol is not believed to be a factor in the crash, which is under investigation.

A Newburyport Building Department inspector also responded and found no structural damage, police said. The restaurant was able to remain open, but the bar are will need to be closed for repairs.

news_boston.title: Recap: Turkey stuns USA 3-2 late in final World Cup group game
news_boston.author: Sanjesh Singh
news_boston.summary:
What to Know

  • Turkey scored the winner in the 98th minute when Kaan Ayhan stabbed home from close range as the USMNT couldn’t clear its lines.
  • Auston Trusty scored in the third minute off a corner kick from Sebastian Berhalter, drilling it home from close range with his left foot. Berhalter later equalized in the 49th minute.
  • Real Madrid young star Arda Guler equalized for Turkey in the 10th minute after getting in behind Mark McKenzie and Miles Robinson. Orkun Kokcu made it 2-1 in the 31st minute.
  • Christian Pulisic came in off the bench in the 58th minute following a calf injury that kept him out of the 2-0 Australia win. He came close to scoring on two occasions.
  • The Stars and Stripes had already won Group D, with Turkey being eliminated from Round of 32 contention following two scoreless losses vs. Australia and Paraguay.
  • The U.S. came in ranked No. 14 by FIFA, with Turkey at No. 32 following a drop of 10 spots from the last ranking.
  • The U.S. will play in the Round of 32 on Wednesday, July 1 at San Francisco Bay Area Stadium, with the opponent being Bosnia and Herzegovina.

This live blog on the USMNT-Turkey World Cup group-stage game has ended. Follow more coverage here.

news_boston.title: How to help those impacted by earthquakes in Venezuela
news_boston.author: Alexis Garcia
news_boston.summary:
Search and rescue efforts are underway in Venezuela after powerful 7.2- and 7.5-magnitude earthquakes struck Wednesday evening, rattling the nation in one of its strongest seismic events in more than a century.

The quakes were felt across the region, leaving at least 164 people dead, more than 1,000 injured and thousands reported missing as officials worked to gauge the full scope of the disaster.

Coastal areas like La Guaira, just north of Caracas, appear to be among the hardest hit, with some of the highest casualties and most extensive damage reported so far, according to officials.

While the full extent of the damage is not yet clear, officials say emergency crews and aid groups have begun mobilizing across the country.

Here are some ways to support people impacted by the earthquakes. Follow the links to learn more about the organizations and how to volunteer or donate.

Venezuela 16 hours ago

Desperate search for survivors under rubble after Venezuela quakes kill at least 235

Venezuela 16 hours ago

Devastating scenes in La Guaira show aftermath of Venezuela earthquakes

Before you give

As always, experts recommend making sure a charity is legitimate before you donate and choosing groups that are well-established. You can find information about them on GuideStar and Charity Navigator.

How to help

Airlink

Airlink is working with airline and logistics partners to mobilize humanitarian partners to help communities across Venezuela by sending: 

  • Search and rescue and emergency medical teams
  • Disaster assessment and management teams
  • Urgently-needed aid, including medical supplies, medicines, water filters, food, shelter supplies and more

With damage to the largest airport in Venezuela, Simón Bolívar International Airport, temporarily pausing flights into the country, Airlink is working to find routes for responders. For more information, click here.

Americares

Americares trains local health workers to help people cope with the trauma of natural disasters and provides medical assistance to health facilities throughout the country. The Americares emergency team, including staff from the region, is assessing urgent health needs for survivors. They are also coordinating their response with the Pan American Health Organization, the United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs and other disaster response organizations. Click here to learn more.

CORE

CORE, or Community Organized Relief Effort, delivers life-saving resources in the immediate aftermath – and empowers communities to be self-reliant in the long run. Its emergency response team is deploying and actively coordinating with trusted partners to assess the widespread needs on the ground. Visit CORE’s website for more information.

Direct Relief

Direct Relief maintains emergency medicines commonly requested during earthquake events, including wound care, surgical supplies, antibiotics, field medic packs to equip first responders and search and rescue crews, and more. The organization will continue to respond to requests in the region as they become known. To discover more about the relief efforts, click here.

Doctors Without Borders/Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF)

Doctors Without Borders/Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) works to help people overcome significant barriers to health care by providing essential medical services and supporting the local health system. MSF teams in Caracas are assessing the situation and donating emergency trauma kits to hospitals. For more information on MSF, visit the website.

Global Empowerment Mission (GEM)

GEM is a disaster relief organization. The GEM team, partnered with I Love Venezuela, is currently mobilizing in response to the developing situation. The reconnaissance and emergency response teams will be assessing needs, coordinating with local partners, and beginning response operations. To learn more about GEM, click here.

International Federation of the Red Cross (IFRC)

The International Federation of the Red Cross (IFRC) said the Venezuelan Red Cross is continuing care in a network of hospitals and polyclinics. Rescue teams are supporting evacuation and search efforts. The Venezuelan Red Cross said its headquarters was among the buildings affected by the earthquake and the group is accounting for the safety of its staff and volunteers. Click here to learn more about IFRC’s relief efforts..

International Rescue Committee (IRC)

IRC teams are on the ground in Venezuela delivering urgent health, nutrition, WASH (water, sanitation and hygiene), protection, education, food security, and livelihood services. For more details on IRC in Venezuela, click here.

Islamic Relief

Islamic Relief is launching an emergency appeal to support those affected. Its members will be working with partners and local authorities to deliver lifesaving aid. For more information, including how to donate, click here.

OCHA

The United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA) is rapidly deploying urban search and rescue teams — highly specialized emergency response units — through the International Search and Rescue Advisory Group, a U.N. advisory body. To discover more about OCHA, visit the website.

Project HOPE

Project HOPE is a U.S.-based international humanitarian aid nonprofit. The organization is mobilizing medical assistance, with international emergency medical personnel and a K-9 search and rescue team on standby. For more information, including how to support, click here.

Save the Children

The international youth humanitarian group says it has already released emergency funds and stands ready to deliver health services, child protection support, shelter, food and essential relief items as needed. Click here to discover more about Save the Children.

World Central Kitchen

Jose Andres’ World Central Kitchen also is on the ground in Venezuela providing food and water to evacuated residents, first responders and tourists. World Center Kitchen typically activates a network of local restaurants, food trucks and emergency kitchens. Visit the website to learn more about World Central Kitchen’s relief efforts.

World Food Program USA

World Food Program USA — and the international program The World Food Programme — delivers lifesaving food aid and is gearing up to support emergency response efforts in Venezuela. For more information including donations, click here.

World Vision

The aid organization World Vision has people in Venezuela and said it was activating its emergency response including:

  • Emergency food assistance to prevent hunger and malnutrition 
  • Clean water and hygiene supplies to reduce the spread of disease 
  • Temporary shelter materials for families who have lost their homes 
  • Medical care and essential supplies for those injured or at risk 
  • Child-friendly spaces to provide safety, stability, and emotional care after trauma 

Click here to learn more about World Vision’s aid efforts.

news_boston.title: Meet the Wada Hoppah, an e-ferry that aims to beat Boston traffic via the Charles
news_boston.author: Eli Rosenberg
news_boston.summary:
A new company is working to put a new kind of rush into Boston’s rush hour.

Inside a warehouse in Concord, Massachusetts, sits the Wada Hoppah, an all-electric ferry designed to carry passengers between Watertown and Boston, giving commuters and visitors a new way to bypass traffic on Storrow Drive, Memorial Drive and other jam-packed roads along the Charles.

“I just can’t wait for Boston to experience this,” said Drew Rollert, the founder of Wada Hoppah.

Rollert gave NBC10 Boston an exclusive first look at the boat, which he hopes to launch in the coming weeks.​ The plan is to connect Watertown to Boston and back​, carrying a dozen or more passengers with stops along the way. Fares will be around $12-$15.

“We have spen​t our entire lives going around the river, and now we are going to save a chunk of time going through it,” Rollert said.

Rollert said the idea came to him after he missed a Red Sox game because of traffic. Since then, he has spent years studying the Charles River and working to build a ferry service that could safely navigate its shallow areas.

“This is literally it. This is V1,” Rollert said while showing off the boat. “There was no template that we could really draw from.”

More on the Wada Hoppah ferry's development

Charles River Aug 30, 2023

Watertown to Boston by ferry? Entrepreneur wants to beat traffic on the Charles

Boston Aug 22, 2024

Can a water shuttle help solve Boston's traffic troubles?

The Wada Hoppah is fully electric and designed to be low wake. Rollert said the goal is to move through the water with as little disruption as possible.

“Anything that comes out the back in terms of wave action will just be ripples, small, small ripples,” he said​, pointing to the ferry’s rotors and patented system.

“You will just be cruising by all that Storrow and Memorial Drive traffic, giggling,” Rollert said. “Nothing against driving, nothing against Ubers, car shares, but the experience is going to blow them out of the water, literally out of the water.”

Rollert said he eventually hopes to operate multiple ferries on the river. He also said the company plans to offer a passenger experience that could include coffee, wine or other beverages on board.

The Department of Conservation and Recreation said it has been working with the company as it develops the concept for a Charles River water shuttle and continues to engage with Rollert’s team. Before the ferry can begin carrying passengers, DCR would need to issue a permit.

According to a DCR spokesperson, that permit would depend on several factors, including an accessibility assessment of the boats and docks being used, the compatibility of the boat with the proposed docks, and details about passenger boarding and disembarking procedures and equipment.

DCR said once it receives all necessary information, the agency will be in a better position to determine next steps.

Rollert said the goal is to get the ferry onto the Charles in the next few days, with passengers potentially on board sometime in early July​.

“The goal is to get as many people out of their cars clogging the city’s roads and onto the river,” Rollert said. “Because once they are on the river, they are not going to go back to their cars. It is just too nice.”

news_bc.title: 90 St. Thomas More To Become Martignetti Hall After Trustee Gift
news_bc.author: Nikita Osadchiy
news_bc.summary:
Boston College will rename 90 St. Thomas More—a sophomore residential hall located on Lower Campus—Martignetti Hall, following a donation from University alumni Beth V. Martignetti, BC ’76, and Trustee Associate Carmine A. Martignetti, BC ’76, the University announced Tuesday.

“The gift, which comes as the couple celebrates 50 years since their BC graduation, will provide unrestricted support to Boston College, allowing the University and its leadership to address its most urgent needs,” a University announcement said. “In grateful recognition of this gift, Boston College will name the residence hall located at 90 Thomas More Drive Martignetti Hall.”

The University did not disclose the size of the couple’s contribution or specify how it would be earmarked.

In the announcement, the couple thanked outgoing University President Rev. William P. Leahy, S.J., for his contributions, and expressed their enthusiasm for the continuity that his successor, Rev. Jack Butler, S.J., will bring when he steps into the role next month.

“We have so much respect for Fr. Leahy and all that he has done for Boston College, which is now among the best Universities in the country thanks to his leadership,” Beth said in the announcement. “Our appreciation doesn’t end there—we’re so delighted for the transition to Fr. Jack Butler and the continuity that he will bring.”

Leahy is set to resign on July 31 following his historic 30-year tenure.

The now-Martignetti Hall was constructed in 1993, alongside its sister residential dorm, Vanderslice Hall. The building contains 60 residential suites and can house up to 380 students, according to the University website. The hall has maintained its residential address as its title until the donation.

Carmine has been a member of the University’s board of trustees since 2015 and is the president of family-owned Martignetti Companies, a wine, spirits, and beer distributor in New England. He and his wife are residents of Chestnut Hill.

Carmine previously served as the chairman of the Tabor Academy Board of Trustees—a college preparatory boarding and day school in Marion, Mass.—from 2014 to 2021, while Beth worked as a surgical nurse at Brigham and Women’s Hospital following graduation from the University.

The Martignettis did not immediately respond to a request for comment made through their company.

The post 90 St. Thomas More To Become Martignetti Hall After Trustee Gift appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: Reimagining Public Media: NPR’s President Talks Its Current State and Its Future
news_bc.author: Mac Cobb
news_bc.summary:
Over the past year, actions taken by President Donald Trump’s administration have framed public media’s role as a divisive and partisan issue. 

But Katherine Maher sees public media as something that should look to bridge social or political divides.

“No one puts a CNN bumper sticker on their car,” Maher said. “There is a deep, emotional relationship that people have with the idea of being someone who participates in the public media.”

Maher, who has served as the president and CEO of National Public Radio (NPR) since 2024, sat down with The Atlantic’s Evan Smith on May 29 as part of The WBUR Festival in Boston.

Before NPR, Maher was the CEO of the Wikimedia Foundation for five years and served as an adviser to the U.S. Secretary of State’s Foreign Affairs Policy Board from 2022 to 2024. 

Maher began her talk, which was moderated by Smith, by discussing the May 2025 defunding of public media by Trump and Congress, which ultimately cut $1.1 billion worth of funding to the Corporation for Public Broadcasting, $120 million of which went to public radio.

“When federal funding was taken off the table by Congress in their decision last year, we knew that there would be a series of consequences,” Maher said.

Those consequences have manifested in recent weeks, as NPR has had to reduce its workforce by 36 people through layoffs, buyouts, or by leaving empty positions unfilled. But Maher is confident that those cuts will end in the near future. 

“You have to do [these cuts] in a way that prevents you from having to go back to that well,” Maher said. “Our goal was to do that. We believe we’ve accomplished that.” 

But with the American public’s trust in media at an all-time low—just 28 percent, according to a recent Gallup poll—Maher sees NPR’s preservation of local media as an important way to keep Americans trusting the news.

“Eighty-five percent of Americans think that local public, not just public media, but local media, is trustworthy and adds value to their community,” Maher said. “The presence of local media is an indicator of lower rates of polarization, greater rates of trust in democratic institutions, but also in your neighbors.” 

With this trust in mind, Maher discussed how people’s decline in radio consumption and changing work habits have forced NPR to adapt its news distribution. 

“We are moving to a model that really encourages digital engagement,” Maher said. “We need to have a digital relationship, because that is where we are today.”

She went on to acknowledge that this movement will force NPR to overcome some of the issues it has had in the past with online coverage.

“There’s a way in the past that the network competed against itself when it came to digital,” she said. “Now my view is that we have to bring everybody together into a singular platform and place where we’re able to say that, you know, all these folks may be consuming information or engaging with everything from Tiny Desk, Morning Edition to your local public affairs show, but they’re doing it in a home that feels like public radio.”

According to Maher, the goal of public media—specifically public radio—is simple: to reduce polarization and get people to recognize each other as people.

“Audio requires you to hear someone—it requires you to listen,” Maher said. “And when we do our work best, we are asking you to listen to someone who you might fundamentally disagree with and also you cannot deny their humanity. That is what public radio can do.”

The post Reimagining Public Media: NPR’s President Talks Its Current State and Its Future appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: BC’s Historic Season Ends With 8–3 Loss to Liberty
news_bc.author: Maria Stefanoudakis
news_bc.summary:
A historic season came to an end on Sunday afternoon as Boston College baseball fell to Liberty for the second time in three days, knocking the Eagles out of the Athens Regional with an 8–3 loss. 

The Eagles, who are coming off the greatest season in program history after winning 17 ACC games and 37 overall, went up 2–0 early in Sunday’s win-or-go-home matchup. But a five-run third inning from Liberty (43–20, 21–9 CUSA) proved too much for BC (37–23, 17–13 ACC) to overcome, and the Eagles exited the game with a trip back to Chestnut Hill looming. 

Ty Mainolfi smacked a home run to right center on just the second at-bat of the game, giving BC an early lead. Brady Miller got off to an efficient start, too, pitching a 1-2-3 first inning. Luke Gallo hit a leadoff double in the bottom of the second, and Gunnar Johnson drove him home with an RBI single. It seemed like the Eagles were rolling. 

Only, that didn’t last. 

Miller retired three batters in order in the bottom of the second, including striking out two. But things started to go south in the third, as a pop up from Jack Toomey stranded Nick Wang on second. The bottom of the frame was when things spiraled out of control. 

A throwing error allowed Liberty to get a runner on second before the Flames had registered a hit. A hit-by-pitch, walk, and a two-RBI single later, Liberty had tied the game. Another BC error—this time in the form of a failed pickoff attempt by Miller—allowed another runner to get home. An RBI triple from Jordan Jaffe and an RBI double from Nick Barone were the icing on the cake. Just like that, Liberty had stolen a 5–2 lead. 

The Flames added on another run in the fourth thanks to a home run from Josh Campos. The Eagles, meanwhile, were unable to generate any offense. Gallo managed a single in the sixth, but that was the Eagles’ only hit after the second inning. 

Liberty scored three more runs across the sixth and seventh innings after Sean Hard relieved Miller. Down 8–2 in the bottom of the seventh, John Kwiatkowski came in for Hard and forced two quick outs to send the game into the eighth. 

Wang and Kyle Wolff both drew walks in the eighth, but a Johnson strikeout ended the frame. Though sophomore transfer John Mitchell was able to silence the Flames in the bottom of the eighth and the Eagles generated one run in the ninth, a Wang flyout ultimately ended BC’s time in Athens—and its season.

The post BC’s Historic Season Ends With 8–3 Loss to Liberty appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: Mudd Leads Eagles to 8–4 Win Over LIU as BC Keeps Playoff Run Alive
news_bc.author: Maria Stefanoudakis
news_bc.summary:
There’s always a moment of silence right before the first pitch. Everyone in the ballpark seems to hold their breath briefly, eagerly awaiting the result as the ball whistles through the air for the first time. 

The silence that came before Tyler Mudd’s first pitch in the Athens Regional game between Boston College baseball and Long Island ended in cheers from the BC dugout. The ball flew perfectly into the strike zone—in retrospect, that moment was a sign of what was to come. 

Mudd pitched 7.1 innings, dealing 10 strikeouts and no walks while giving up seven hits and four runs. Four of those hits and three of those runs came in the eighth inning, when Long Island (30–22, 26–7 Northeast) staged a small rally and cut the Eagles’ (37–22, 17–13 Atlantic Coast) lead to four. But BC pulled through and captured an 8–4 win, keeping their postseason hopes alive following a loss to Liberty on Friday. 

“I know we had gotten the offensive [going] early, but I thought the story of the game was Tyler [Mudd] going as far as he did,” BC head coach Todd Interdonato said. “It was just nice for us to get our style of play on the field.”

After scoring only three runs—all off solo shots—in Friday’s loss, the Eagles found a more balanced offensive approach in Saturday’s noon matchup on Foley Field. 

“I think that’s kind of what we’ve been preaching, especially the past two or three weeks—obviously, things haven’t gone great offensively, but we just kept on preaching about finding our rhythm within our offense, because that’s what we’ve done all year,” BC’s Carter Hendrickson said. 

Julio Solier, the man of the hour after his two home runs on Friday, remained hot, leading off with a line drive double that bounced its way through the gaps in the center field defense with precision. A Ty Mainolfi sacrifice groundout advanced Solier to third, and an RBI single from Nick Wang drove home the Eagles’ first run of the day in the top of the first inning. 

Mudd retired the first seven batters he faced before finally giving up a single in the bottom of the third. But when asked about his performance, the graduate pitcher out of Shrewsberry, Mass. was eager to credit his teammates. 

“The defense behind me played unbelievable. Luke [Gallo] made that diving play early. I don’t think that stats really show, [LIU] hit some balls hard,” Mudd said. “[My teammates] just played unbelievable defensively and got up to an early lead, so it made my job very easy.”

Meanwhile, BC’s offense extended its lead, starting with an RBI sacrifice bunt from Ben Williams and a two-RBI home run from Hendrickson in the second inning. 

“I feel like we were creating a lot of momentum today,” Hendrickson said. “Especially in pregame, a lot of our talk was, ‘Okay, once we find our rhythm, it’s going to be hard to stop us.’ I feel like today we really stacked a lot of at-bats on top of each other, and that’s why we’ve had so much success this season. I think we kind of found our rhythm back today.” 

The Eagles tacked on three more runs in the top of the fourth, starting with Solier hit by a pitch that brought Kyle Wolff home. In the next at-bat, Mainolfi’s bat struck gold for a two-RBI single. BC’s final run of the day came in the fifth, when Jack Toomey hit his second home run in as many games to make it 8–0 Eagles.

After dealing five strikeouts, Mudd finally gave up a run in the bottom of the fifth when Joseph Durso hit a solo shot over the wall in left field. Neither team scored again until the eighth—but when Long Island’s bats finally started finding the ball, BC faltered. The dam broke when Ryan Rivera hammered a three-run home run, cutting the Eagles’ lead to 8–4 and prompting Interdonato to end Mudd’s day at 7.1 innings. 

Chase Hartsell headed to the mound, but gave up a walk on the first at-bat he faced and was immediately replaced by Kyle Kipp. Kipp struck out two batters looking, ending the frame and hindering Long Island’s chances at a comeback. BC’s offense was silent in the ninth, but its defense pulled through once again in the bottom of the frame, holding the lead to come away with an 8–4 win.

The post Mudd Leads Eagles to 8–4 Win Over LIU as BC Keeps Playoff Run Alive appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: BC Falls to Liberty 4–3 After Ninth-Inning Collapse in Opening Game of NCAA Tournament
news_bc.author: Maria Stefanoudakis
news_bc.summary:
Boston College baseball isn’t known as a hard-hitting team—far from it, in fact. The Eagles hit the fewest home runs in the ACC this season (40), 32 fewer than the second-lowest team. 

But the iron-clad identity the Eagles have sported all season, often defined by small ball and a distinct ability to run, came into question on Friday afternoon in Athens, Ga. BC scored all three of its runs off solo shots, including two from Julio Solier and one from Jack Toomey. 

The hard-hitting magic wasn’t enough for the Eagles (36–22, 17–13 Atlantic Coast) to knock off Liberty, however. The Flames (42–19, 21–9 Conference-USA) took a 4–3 win in the opening game of the Athens Regional thanks to two ninth-inning runs, as BC lost the first NCAA Tournament game it has played in since 2023. 

“I thought the difference when I look at it, I could be wrong, I don’t think we got the leadoff guy on until the ninth,” BC head coach Todd Interdonato said. 

After hitting back-to-back RBI singles in the first inning, the Flames maintained a 2–0 lead through the top of the fifth, as starting pitcher Ben Blair dealt seven strikeouts over 7.0 innings pitched. The bottom of the fifth didn’t start much differently for the Eagles, as Carter Hendrickson grounded out to the shortstop on a loaded count. Then, Solier stepped up to the plate. 

Despite leading the Eagles in hits (80), Solier hit only one home run all season. On Friday, though, he caught fire. His first homer of the day flew over the wall in center field, cutting Liberty’s lead to 2–1. He did it again in the bottom of the seventh, sending the ball soaring just over the Georgia logo on the outfield wall to tie the game 2–2. 

“I just like winning. That’s all that matters to me,” Solier said. “I put my praise to God and hit those home runs for the team, not only for me. I play for them. I don’t really play for me.”

While the Eagles—except for Solier, that is—struggled offensively, BC’s defense kept the game in reach. Starting senior A.J. Colarusso recovered after Liberty’s two-run opening frame, and did not allow another score in his 7.0 innings pitched. 

“I thought A.J. in the seventh was electric getting out of there,” Interdonato said. “We were having a conversation about letting him go back out.” 

But Gavin Soares entered in the top of the eighth, and struck out three in another fruitless frame for the Flames. 

After Nick Wang led off the bottom of the eighth with a fly out to center field on the first pitch of his at-bat, Toomey finally broke the 2–2 stalemate. He sent a line drive home run into the corner in right field, giving BC its first lead of the day. That was all the offense the Eagles could muster, but it was enough to carry them into the final frame up 3–2. 

That didn’t last long, though. 

Liberty led off with back-to-back base hits to put two runners in scoring position. An RBI groundout from Jordan Jaffe tied the game 3–3 and placed Riley DeCandido on third base, threatening a go-ahead run. BC looked like it would tag DeCandido out on the following play, as Solier sent the ball to catcher Gunnar Johnson on a fielder’s choice. But the throw flew right of Johnson’s head and DeCandido got home safely, giving Liberty a one-run lead. 

As they had all game, the Eagles struggled offensively in the ninth. Johnson was hit by a pitch, then pinch runner Esteban Garcia advanced to second on a sacrifice groundout from Ben Williams. But back-to-back fly outs from Hendrickson and Solier ended the frame, and the Eagles lost their NCAA Tournament opener. 

“It’s a really good game,” Interdonato said. “It just hurts being on this end of it.”

The post BC Falls to Liberty 4–3 After Ninth-Inning Collapse in Opening Game of NCAA Tournament appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: Trump Announces He Will Posthumously Award Welles Crowther Presidential Medal of Freedom
news_bc.author: Nikita Osadchiy
news_bc.summary:
President Donald Trump announced he would posthumously award Welles Crowther, BC ’99, with the Presidential Medal of Freedom—one of the nation’s highest civilian honors—during a campaign rally in New York Friday evening.

“We are approaching the 25th anniversary of September 11, 2001, a dark day that will live in infamy,” Trump said. “We are posthumously awarding Welles the Presidential Medal of Freedom—which is the highest award outside of the Congressional Medal of Honor.”

Trump’s announcement came several months after Congressman Mike Lawler (R-N.Y.) publicly urged the president to recognize Crowther’s heroism.

The president traveled to Rockland County, N.Y.—Crowther’s hometown—to campaign for Lawler in his competitive district.

Crowther was a volunteer firefighter. On the morning of Sept. 11, 2001, Crowther was working as an equities trader in the South Tower of the World Trade Center. 

After two hijacked passenger planes struck the Twin Towers, he led a group of survivors from the 78th floor down to safety, helping save as many as 18 lives. 

He chose to rush back into the burning tower in an effort to rescue more people. When the tower collapsed, Crowther was killed. He was 24. 

Survivors later identified Crowther as “the man in the red bandanna,” a reference to the distinctive handkerchief he wore on that day while helping others escape.

The president congratulated Welles’ mother, Alison Crowther, who stood onstage beside him wearing Welles’ signature red bandanna around her neck.

“I just want to congratulate his great mother in doing a phenomenal job in raising that young man,” Trump said. “Boy, what bravery saved those people, and [he] became a legend. No one else would have done what he did.”

Alison Crowther thanked the president before addressing the crowd with her own remarks.

“To me, it is such a beautiful thing that even 25 years later, Welles’ light still shines brightly,” she said. “I travel around the country—and I’ve even been to Amman, Jordan—talking about Welles’ story, and his legacy and his example of courage, fearlessness … and his pure humanity that day saving so many lives and as I travel and speak to children, they’re tremendously moved and inspired by this to be better people.”

Alison Crowther also thanked The New York Times for uncovering Welles’ story.

Welles Crowther’s legacy remains deeply embedded in the Boston College community. Every fall, the University honors him with the annual Red Bandanna Game, commemorating his sacrifice in saving the lives of others during the attack.

His name is also engraved on the 9/11 Memorial Labyrinth outside Burns Library, on BC’s campus, alongside 21 other alumni who lost their lives that day.

During his announcement, Trump did not state when the award ceremony would take place.

The post Trump Announces He Will Posthumously Award Welles Crowther Presidential Medal of Freedom appeared first on The Heights.
